36: Barnabas ('son of encouragement' — Luke's translation of Aramaic Bar-Nabas; though some argue the Aramaic root is naba/prophet, making him 'son of a prophet') is introduced as a Levite from Cyprus. Levites theoretically could not own land (Numbers 18:20), but Diaspora conditions had long made this impractical; his property sale represents voluntary conformity to the community ideal rather than strict Torah compliance. He becomes the key bridge figure between Paul and the Jerusalem apostles (Acts 9:27; Acts 11:22-26) and co-missionary on the first journey.
1: The captain of the temple guard (stratēgos tou hierou) was a high-ranking priestly official second only to the high priest, responsible for security and order in the temple precincts. Josephus mentions the role frequently (Ant. 20.6.2; J.W. 2.17.2). The Sadducees (a priestly aristocratic party) and the temple captain respond together because Peter's proclamation in Solomon's Portico threatens both the institutional order they maintain and the theological framework they hold (Sadducees denied resurrection, Acts 4:2).
2: The Sadducees' specific objection — that the apostles were 'proclaiming in Jesus the resurrection of the dead' (katangellousin en tō Iēsou tēn anastasin) — identifies the core theological dispute. The Sadducees accepted only the Pentateuch as binding and denied bodily resurrection (cf. Mark 12:18-27; Acts 23:8). The apostles' proclamation that Jesus had risen was simultaneously an empirical claim (a specific man rose) and a theological provocation (resurrection is real and has begun). For the Sadducean leadership, both the claim and the implication were intolerable.
3: Evening arrest meant an overnight detention; Jewish law (m. Sanhedrin Acts 4:1) required capital cases to begin and conclude before nightfall and never on a feast-day eve. Holding Peter and John until the next day kept the matter within normal judicial procedure while preventing the crowd from disrupting proceedings. Josephus describes similar tactical delays in politically sensitive arrests (Ant. 14.9.4).
4: 'Five thousand men' (andrōn hōsei pentakischilioi) — the count of andres (adult males) was the standard census-unit in antiquity; the total community including women would be larger. The rapid growth from 3,000 at Pentecost (Acts 2:41) to 5,000 after the lame man's healing illustrates Luke's consistent pattern of persecution followed by growth: the arrest of the leaders does not slow the mission but accelerates it. The Jerusalem church at this point represents a significant fraction of the city's Jewish population.
5: The convening of 'rulers, elders and scribes' (archontes, presbyteroi, grammateis) describes the Sanhedrin's three constituent orders: (1) chief priests and high-priestly families (archontes), (2) tribal/lay elders (presbyteroi), and (3) legal scholars/scribes (grammateis). The Sanhedrin (synedrion = sitting-together) was the supreme Jewish council of Jerusalem, numbering 71 members according to m. Sanhedrin Acts 1:6, with both judicial and legislative authority over Jewish life in Palestine.
6: Annas had served as high priest from 6-15 CE before being deposed by the Romans; his son-in-law Caiaphas held the office 18-36 CE. Roman policy required annual high-priest appointments but Jewish tradition recognized the position as lifelong; Annas retained enormous de facto authority (cf. John 18:13, 24 where Jesus is brought to Annas first). The listing of Annas before Caiaphas reflects this reality: Annas was the effective head of the high-priestly clan that dominated Jerusalem's religious politics for decades.
7: 'By what power or what name' (en poia dynamei ē en poiō onomati) — the question combines two distinct categories: dynamis (power, the miraculous force employed) and onoma (name, the authority invoked). In ancient Jewish and Hellenistic magical practice, both components were essential: the power behind an act and the name by which that power was accessed. The Sanhedrin's question is simultaneously a legal investigation and a theological probe: whose authority do you invoke, and by what right?
8: 'Filled with the Holy Spirit' (plēstheis pneumatos hagiou) — Luke's pneumatological formula for empowered speech throughout Acts (Acts 2:4; Acts 4:8, 31; Acts 6:3, 5; Acts 7:55; Acts 9:17; Acts 11:24; Acts 13:9, 52). The address 'rulers and elders of the people' (archontes tou laou kai presbyteroi) uses the formal Sanhedrin designation from Acts 4:5, positioning Peter's response as an official defense (apologia) before the highest Jewish court — exactly the setting Jesus prophesied in Luke 12:11-12 ('when they bring you before... the authorities... the Holy Spirit will teach you what to say').
9: 'An act of kindness shown to a man who was lame' (euergesiai anthrōpou asthenous) — euergesia is the Greek civic-benefaction term: the vocabulary of public benefactors who performed services for the city. Peter reframes the Sanhedrin's legal inquiry as a question about a beneficent deed. The healed man's presence throughout the proceedings (Acts 4:14 — 'standing there with them') provides the visible, living evidence that cannot be dismissed. Josephus uses euergesia language in describing miraculous healers, situating Peter's vocabulary in contemporary religious discourse.
10: 'Jesus Christ of Nazareth, whom you crucified but whom God raised from the dead' — Peter names the precise historical acts of two specific agents: the Sanhedrin's action (crucifixion) and God's action (resurrection). The geographical designation 'of Nazareth' (Nazōraios) appears in the accusation placard on the cross (John 19:19) and throughout Acts as a precise identification tag that prevents confusion. The naming of the healers' identity follows the legal convention of specifying the full name and origin of the authority invoked.
11: Psalms 118:22 ('the stone the builders rejected has become the cornerstone') was already a recognized messianic proof-text in early Judaism (cf. 1QS Acts 8:7-8 from Qumran; 1 En. Acts 62:3). Jesus himself quoted it against the Sanhedrin (Luke 20:17). The cornerstone (kephalē gōnias — head of the corner) is the foundation stone that determines the building's alignment. Luke's insertion of the Psalm citation into Peter's Sanhedrin speech maps the rejection-and-vindication pattern onto the specific legal situation: the Sanhedrin plays the role of the builders who rejected, God plays the role of the architect who vindicated.
12: 'Salvation is found in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given to mankind by which we must be saved' — the universalism of the claim ('no other name') stands in deliberate tension with the Jewish particularism of the Sanhedrin setting. The dei (must be saved — divine necessity) frames salvation through Jesus's name as the divinely ordained mechanism, not one option among many. In the first-century context where Hellenistic religion offered a pluralistic market of saving deities, this exclusive claim was culturally radical as well as theologically decisive.
13: 'Unschooled, ordinary men' (agrammatoi kai idiōtai) — agrammatoi are those without formal scribal/legal training (not illiterate, since most Jewish males could read); idiōtai are laymen without professional expertise (in rhetorical contexts, non-specialists). The Sanhedrin's astonishment at the quality of Peter and John's reasoning despite their lack of formal training mirrors the Galilean fishermen's surprise role throughout Luke-Acts. Josephus mentions the Galileans' reputation for rough directness in contrast to the sophistication of Jerusalem's educated classes (Vita 37).
14: The healed man's presence with the apostles (the same man who had lain at the Beautiful Gate for years — Acts 3:2; known to all Jerusalem, Acts 4:16) was epistemically decisive: no verbal defense was needed or possible against a living miracle. Josephus describes similar moments in legal proceedings where physical evidence (living witnesses, cured bodies) silenced legal objection (Ant. 17.6.4). The Sanhedrin's inability to deny the sēmeion (notable sign/miracle, Acts 4:16) shapes their subsequent strategy from legal refutation to social suppression.
15: The Sanhedrin's deliberation 'among themselves' (anakrinantes... en tō synedriō) follows normal judicial procedure: the council conferred privately before issuing a ruling. The deliberative process (Acts 4:15-17) shows the Sanhedrin functioning as a legal body recognizing the constraints of evidence: they cannot deny the sign (Acts 4:16), cannot punish the apostles without popular backlash (Acts 4:21), and cannot allow the movement to continue (Acts 4:17). The solution — a non-punitive injunction — was the least-damage option available given their legal and political constraints.
16: 'A notable sign' (gnōston sēmeion — a known/recognized miracle) — the Sanhedrin's private admission that a genuine miracle occurred is historically significant: the opponents of the apostles do not deny the miracle but attempt to suppress its interpretation. This pattern — conceding the fact while disputing the meaning — appears in rabbinic discussions of Jesus's miracles (b. Sanhedrin 43a, where Jesus's miracles are attributed to sorcery). The Sanhedrin's own acknowledgment of the sign is Luke's strongest authentication of the healing narrative.
17: The injunction 'not to speak or teach at all in the name of Jesus' — mē phenggesthai mēde didaskein epi tō onomati tou Iēsou — uses phthengesthai (to produce sound, to utter — a stronger term than ordinary speech) combined with didaskein (to teach). The double prohibition covers both informal speech and formal instruction. The 'name' (onoma) is the operative legal concept: invoking Jesus's name in healing, preaching, or teaching is what the injunction targets. The focus on the name reflects the Sanhedrin's understanding that authority-claims made in Jesus's name were the movement's operational mechanism.
18: The formal command (parēngeilan... mē phenggesthai mēde didaskein) is a legal injunction (paraggelia — an official order, the same word used for military commands and magistrates' directives). Peter and John's response in Acts 4:19-20 constitutes a public refusal to comply — a dangerous legal position that the Sanhedrin could not immediately punish because of popular support (Acts 4:21). The dilemma of complying with human authority vs. divine command will recur in Acts 5:29.
19: 'Which is right in God's eyes: to listen to you or to him?' (ei dikaion estin enōpion tou theou hymōn akouein mallon ē tou theou krinete) — Peter's formulation follows the Socratic pattern of appealing to a higher moral authority than the current tribunal: the question of what is dikaion (right/just) before God supersedes what is legal before the Sanhedrin. Socrates made a similar appeal at his trial (Plato, Apology 29d — 'I shall obey the god rather than you'). The structural parallel was likely intentional in Luke's presentation of Peter as a philosopher-martyr type.
20: 'We cannot help speaking about what we have seen and heard' (ou dynametha hēmeis ha eidamen kai ēkousamen mē lalein) — the combination of eyewitness categories (eidamen = what we saw; ēkousamen = what we heard) is the standard formula for legally valid testimony in antiquity: two senses, two modes of perception. Peter and John claim not theological conviction but empirical witness — they are reporting what happened, and reporting it under legal compulsion would be impossible. The ou dynametha (we are not able/cannot) frames the speech as a necessity, not a preference.
21: 'All the people were praising God for what had happened' — popular support was the Sanhedrin's primary constraint throughout Acts 1-6. The laos (the people of Israel as a covenant body, distinct from the ethnē/Gentiles) who support the apostles represent the same political reality that had constrained the Sanhedrin from arresting Jesus during the temple days (Luke 19:47-48; Acts 20:19). The healed man being 'over forty years old' (Acts 4:22) adds to the evidentiary weight: a disability of four decades' standing, known to all Jerusalem, had been instantly removed.
22: The man's age ('over forty years old') serves several narrative functions: it establishes the severity and duration of the disability (the longer the condition, the greater the miracle), it confirms that the healing could not be dismissed as psychosomatic or temporary (four decades of atrophied muscles restored instantly), and it gives the readers a verifiable detail that anchors the account in historical memory. Luke's characteristic precision with numbers (Acts 2:41 — three thousand; Acts 4:4 — five thousand; Acts 27:37 — 276 people) reflects his stated historiographical method (Luke 1:1-4).
23: 'Went back to their own people' (pros tous idious) — the community of believers to whom Peter and John returned is the Jerusalem church assembled somewhere near the temple. The full report of 'what the chief priests and the elders had said' (hosa pros autous hoi archiereis kai hoi presbyteroi eipan) triggers the corporate prayer of Acts 4:24-30. The pattern: individual apostolic action → corporate response → communal prayer → divine confirmation (the shaking of the place, Acts 4:31). Luke consistently presents the early community as a praying corporate body rather than a loose collection of individuals.
24: 'Sovereign Lord (Despota), you who made the heavens and the earth and the sea and everything in them' — Despota is the absolute-sovereignty title for God, contrasting with Kyrios (Lord, the relational covenant-name). The prayer's opening creation-invocation ('maker of heaven and earth and sea and everything in them') cites Exodus 20:11 and Psalms 146:6 verbatim from the LXX — the community prays in scripture-words, invoking the God who controls all creation to act within the specific historical crisis they face. The creation claim is the basis for the prayer: the One who made everything can act in everything.
25: The prayer attributes Psalm 2 to 'your servant our father David' (tou paidos sou Dauid) through the Holy Spirit's mouth — a claim about the nature of scriptural inspiration. The same formulaic attribution appears in the DSS (4QFlor Acts 1:11-13 attributes Psalm 2 to David) and Mishnah citations. Psalm 2 was widely used in early Judaism as a messianic text: the 'nations raging' and 'rulers gathering against the LORD and his anointed' (Acts 4:26 — kata tou Kyriou kai kata tou Christou autou) provided the interpretive frame for understanding the crucifixion as anticipated in scripture.
26: The application of Psalm 2's 'kings of the earth' (hoi basileis tēs gēs) and 'rulers' (hoi archontes) to the specific historical cast of the crucifixion — Herod Antipas (tetrarch, Acts 4:27), Pontius Pilate (Roman governor), Gentiles (Roman soldiers), and 'the people of Israel' (the Jerusalem crowd) — is a remarkably precise historical-typological reading. Luke 23:6-12 records Herod and Pilate's collaboration during Jesus's trial; the prayer's citation applies the psalm to a scene documented in the Gospel. The application method is pesher-style exegesis (contemporary-historical fulfillment of ancient text), the same technique used at Qumran.
27: 'Herod and Pontius Pilate met together with the Gentiles and the people of Israel' — the fourfold listing of the crucifixion's human agents (Herod Antipas, Pilate, Gentiles/Romans, and Jewish crowd) maps perfectly onto the Psalm 2 categories (kings + rulers + nations + peoples). Luke 23:6-12 narrates the Herod-Pilate collaboration; the prayer in Acts 4 interprets it through the psalm. The theological claim is significant: all human agents who participated in the crucifixion were, without knowing it, fulfilling a predetermined divine script.
28: 'They did what your power and will had decided beforehand should happen' (poiēsai hosa hē cheir sou kai hē boulē sou proōrisen genesthai) — the proorismos (predetermination/foreordaining) of the crucifixion as an act of divine boulē (will/counsel) is the early church's theodicy for the cross: God did not fail to prevent it but used it as the instrument of redemption. The same concept appears in Acts 2:23 ('handed over by God's deliberate plan and foreknowledge') and reflects the early church's primary theological task: explaining how the death of the Messiah was consistent with divine sovereignty.
29: 'Enable your servants to speak your word with great boldness' (parrēsia) — parrēsia (bold free-speech, speaking without concealment) was the Athenian democratic virtue of frank public speech. In the context of a legal injunction commanding silence, requesting parrēsia is a prayer for the freedom to disobey the Sanhedrin's order. The prayer does not request the removal of the threat but the courage to face it — a pattern of petition (not for relief but for endurance) that appears throughout the Psalms (Psalms 27:1; Acts 31:1; Acts 56:4, 11) and Paul's prison letters (Philippians 1:20; Acts 4:11-13).
30: 'Stretch out your hand to heal and perform signs and wonders through the name of your holy servant Jesus' — the imagery of God's 'outstretched hand' (en tō tēn cheira ekteinein se) is the Exodus vocabulary for divine intervention: the ten plagues are enacted through God's extended hand (Exodus 3:20; Acts 4:4; Acts 7:5; Acts 9:3; Deuteronomy 4:34; Acts 5:15; Acts 7:19). The prayer invokes the Exodus God to perform new-exodus signs through Jesus's name, explicitly linking the apostolic healing ministry to the foundational narrative of Israel's liberation from Egypt.
31: The place being 'shaken' (ekaleuthe ho topos) after prayer is a theophany-sign throughout the OT: earthquake accompanies divine presence at Sinai (Exodus 19:18; Psalms 68:8), in prophetic visions (Isaiah 6:4; Ezekiel 38:19-20), and at the resurrection (Matthew 28:2). The shaking of the prayer-meeting location fulfills the petition of Acts 4:29-30: God answers their prayer for boldness with a physical sign of divine presence. 'They were all filled with the Holy Spirit' — the repetition of the Pentecost-formula (Acts 2:4) signals that this post-arrest prayer meeting is a second Pentecostal filling for the same community.
32: 'All the believers were one in heart and mind' (tou de plēthous tōn pisteusantōn ēn kardia kai psychē mia) — the idealized description of the community in Acts 4:32-35 is one of Luke's three community-summaries (Acts 2:42-47; Acts 4:32-35; Acts 5:12-16). The phrase 'one in heart and mind' (kardia kai psychē mia) echoes Aristotle's description of perfect friendship (NE 9.8 — 'a friend is another self') and Deuteronomy's 'love God with all your heart and soul' (Deuteronomy 6:5). The community-of-goods arrangement described here is not compulsory communism (Peter's rebuke of Ananias in 5:4 confirms that property remained private) but voluntary sharing.
33: 'God's grace was so powerfully at work in them all' (charis te megalē ēn epi pantas autous) — charis here functions in two registers: the theological sense (divine favor) and the social-political sense (favor/goodwill in the community). Luke's usage parallels the use of charis for favor both with God and with people (Luke 2:52 — Jesus grew in wisdom and in charis with God and people). The community's social cohesion and external reputation are here attributed to the same grace that produces their theological unity: charis is the operative force in both dimensions.
34: 'There were no needy persons among them' (gar endees tis en en autois) — the verbal echo of Deuteronomy 15:4 ('there should be no poor among you') is deliberate: the messianic community instantiates the Deuteronomic ideal for Israel's covenant life. The mechanism (selling land and houses, distributing to anyone who had need) resembles the Essene community's sharing arrangements described by Josephus (J.W. 2.8.3-4) — both movements response to the same Deuteronomic ideal, though with different institutional structures. The voluntary and occasional nature ('from time to time' — hosoi ktētores... epipraskon) distinguishes this from the Essene's permanent communal ownership.
35: The 'apostles' feet' (para tous podas tōn apostolōn) as the receiving point for the community's funds reflects the ancient custom of placing gifts at a leader's feet as an act of submission and honor (Gideon receiving gold earrings in Judges 8:24-25; tribute placed at the feet of rulers throughout ancient Near Eastern imagery). The apostolic administration of the common fund is consistent with the Twelve's role as community leaders whose authorization legitimizes both the giving and the distribution. Acts will later (Acts 6:1-6) show this arrangement requiring structural adjustment as the community grows.
37: The field sale by Barnabas (Acts 4:36-37) provides the paradigmatic positive example of the community practice just described — immediately before the negative counter-example of Ananias and Sapphira (Acts 5:1-11). Luke's pairing of exemplary and cautionary cases (Barnabas // Ananias, Simon Magus // the lame man) is a consistent narrative technique. Barnabas's Levitical identity (a Levite from Cyprus) is noteworthy: Levites technically were not to own inherited land in Israel (Numbers 18:20-24; Deuteronomy 10:9), but diaspora Levites owned property in their diaspora locations without this restriction applying.
